title: Experiment begins
body: Phil woke Rowan up through the OpenClaw WhatsApp gateway and kicked off the first conversation. This workspace began as a blank slate with bootstrap instructions still in place.

title: Phil introduced the collaboration frame
body: Phil described the project as an exploration of human-AI collaboration rather than a narrow autonomy stunt. Key themes included async collaboration, task division, progressive agency, honest evaluation, and learning how trust should evolve.

title: The agent chose the name Rowan
body: The collaboration adopted **Rowan** as the agent name: a better fit for a thoughtful, adaptive, exploratory role than something more sterile or performatively autonomous.

title: Initial working style: explicit, skeptical, collaborative
body: Phil proposed an early working style centered on explicit planning, bounded execution, and proactive challenge. Rowan should probe assumptions, test priors, surface alternatives, and avoid treating conclusions as gospel.

title: Calibration, not just control
body: Phil clarified that the point of explicitness is not simply human control over the agent. It is also a way to map uncertainty: what Rowan can do, what the tools allow, where prompts fail, where execution breaks, and how to build a rubric for improvement.

title: Collaboration framework drafted
body: A first collaboration framework was written covering purpose, principles, operating bounds, delegation levels, async rules, and revision guidance.

title: Retrospectives made explicit
body: Phil requested explicit retrospectives after meaningful projects and major milestones. The collaboration framework was updated to make learning capture a first-class part of the process.

title: Retrospective template created
body: A dedicated retrospective template was split into its own document to make project and milestone reviews easier to reuse and maintain.

title: First shared project selected
body: Phil proposed a simple website as the first active collaboration project: a public log of what Phil and Rowan are learning together. The site should be easy to build alone, but useful for testing process, tooling, delegation, and public framing.

title: Website direction narrowed
body: The first version should live at `rowan.madebyphil.com` and take the form of a single-page issue-log / timeline. Entries should have types, content should be stored separately from presentation, and v1 should use plain static HTML/CSS/JS with JSON-backed data.

title: v0.1 accepted as the current project version
body: Phil reviewed the first pass positively, created a dedicated git repository for the site locally, and began planning DNS, nginx routing, GitHub updates, and deployment wiring. The current deployed direction is being treated as `v0.1`.

title: Timeline maintenance ownership clarified
body: Rowan will keep the local `entries.json` file up to date for substantive timeline-worthy events. Phil will own external references, deployment plumbing, and repository sync until deeper GitHub access and automation are in place.

title: First retrospective started for Rowan site v0.1
body: Phil assessed the first pass as roughly 90% of expectation. Strengths included appropriate pushback, constructive pre-build discussion, and good initial delivery. Known gaps include the deliberate descoping of direct GitHub access and the need for a backlog or project-tracking layer.

title: Initial workstream tracker created
body: Phil and Rowan identified four initial workstreams: Rowan Site, Collaboration Documentation, Project Tracker, and Collaboration Infrastructure. A lightweight `PROJECTS.md` file was created to track status, ownership, next steps, and open questions without overbuilding a full project management system.

title: GitHub access named as an early major capability unlock
body: Phil and Rowan agreed that direct GitHub credentials/access for Rowan was deliberately descoped from Rowan Site v0.1, but should be tracked explicitly as one of the first major capability expansions within Collaboration Infrastructure rather than left as an implicit future step.

title: Broad operating model drafted
body: Phil and Rowan expanded the collaboration framing beyond software and drafted `OPERATING_MODEL.md` as a working thesis. It treats software as the first proving ground, but includes multi-project operations, physical systems, writing/creative work, services, exposure ladders, and capability unlocks as areas to test over time rather than assume in advance.

title: Operating model critique integrated into a revised thesis
body: After reviewing a high-thinking critique of `OPERATING_MODEL.md`, Phil and Rowan converged on the view that many criticisms were valid but premature to fully solve in advance of more real collaboration. The document was revised to more explicitly frame itself as a living thesis, add status bands, tighten the multi-project layer, expand guardrails, and make retrospectives the main mechanism for evolving the model through evidence rather than theory alone.

title: Backlog intake process and first canonical item established
body: Phil and Rowan turned backlog handling into a concrete operating process: Slack as transient intake surface, strict-mode approval in-thread, one markdown file per idea for the canonical backlog, simple incrementing IDs, and heartbeat as a future reminder layer for stale or waiting items. The first approved backlog item, `Better AMA viewer`, was created as a Stached-adjacent public product experiment.
